titleStmt.title: Letter from John Chamberlain to Dudley Carleton, 17 May 1598

publicationStmt.p: Published digitally at earlymodernletters.org. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

div.p: You have gotten the start so far before me, that I am almost out of hope to overtake you, and yet I have not bene altogether negligent, for this is my second letter since my comming to towne about the beginning of this moneth, but I doubt mine do not finde such spedy passage as yours do hitherwards, for I receved one of the 10th upon the 13th of this present, and another of the 12th yesterday. I delivered my last at Master WilliamsPresumably Alexander Williams. to be sent with the first opportunity, but I understand by Mistris AliceAlice Carleton. yt was there within these three dayes: this (according to your direction) I send by Master ClarkeEdward Clarke, appointed Clerk of the Council in 1603, and later in the service of George Villiers, first Duke of Buckingham., whom I could not heare of at your Lord GovernorsSir Edward Norris, Governor of Ostend, third son of Henry Lord Norris of Rycote., but I caused my man to finde him out in Litle Woodstreet, but (by his sayeng) he made more questions and examined him upon more interrogatories then the conveying of a letter comes to, so that yf you have any better meanes let me know yt, and I will not fayle you from time to time while I am here.

We are all of opinion that the peace is forward and the Lord BuckhurstThomas Sackville, Lord Buckhurst (created Earl of Dorset, 1604). is appointed to go to Abbeville or some other place thereabout to conclude yt. Master BodleyThomas Bodley (knighted, 1604), founder of the Bodleian Library. is named as an assistant, for countenance, but he will not go yf he can possiblie avoyde yt. There is speach likewise of some greater man goes as the Lord Marquis of WinchesterWilliam Paulet, third Marquis of Winchester., or the erle of NorthumberlandHenry Percy, ninth Earl of Northumberland. or the erle of ShrewsburieGilbert Talbot, seventh Earl of Shrewsbury.. Matters in Ireland grow dayly worse and worse, so that unles they have round and speedy succoures all is like to go to wracke; the counsayle have consulted about yt these three or fowre dayes, but I heare of no resolution but only that 4000 men shalbe sent at leysure. Here go flienge tales that the erle of CumberlandGeorge Clifford, third Earl of Cumberland, sailed from Portsmouth March 6, 1598. He intercepted no treasure ships. (G. C. Williamson, George, Third Earl of Cumberland, p. 179.) hath met with fowre carraques bounde for the Indies and suncke one of them and taken two, but yt is so diversly and faintly reported that I have no great beliefe in yt. We say that the Hollanders have taken a rich ship of treasure comming out of Spaine that past by our loytering sentinells that lie about the Downes. The Countesse of HartfordFrances, daughter of William, first Lord Howard of Effingham, and second wife of Edward Seymour, Earl of Hertford, died May 14. is lately dead, and mine old frend Tom Powell whose companie I shall much want for my walke in Powles. Sir Charles Blunt stayed behinde Master Secretary in Fraunce about a quarrell he had with a Dutchman that was knighted at CalesSir Melchior Leven, knighted by Essex at Cadiz, refused to fight Sir Charles Blount in Paris on the ground that the King had forbidden duels. (Salisbury MSS., viii, 168, 219, 224–231.) that had challenged him; newes came two dayes since that they have met and that the Duchman is slaine.

Our goode frend Master Evers hath at last left Irland and came to towne last weeke; within three dayes after, his cosen the Lord EversRalph, third Lord Evers, Warden of the Middle Marches. In November, 1596, he wrote Lord Burghley, and in January, 1598, he wrote to Sir Robert Cecil and again to Lord Burghley to complain of the insolence and riotous conduct of the Widdringtons or Woodringtons. On January 22 they were summoned to appear before the Privy Council, and a month later they reached London. On May 14 the Council wrote the Mayor, Recorder, and Sheriffs of London to investigate the "affraye betwixt our very good Lord the Lord Eure and his brother and certain of the Woodringtons, gentlemen of the Northe Countrey." (Salisbury MSS., viii, 73; Border Papers, 226, 506; A. P. C., xxviii, 279, 328, 443.) and his brother Sir William walking the streets with one page after them, were set upon (as they say) by fowre or five of the Witheringtons about a country quarrell, and so overlayde that the Lord was hurt in the hande, and Sir William receved divers sore wounds as in the hande, in the shoulder, over the face, and quite thorough the body, which he holdes out yet being the fift day and I hope will do.

Dr. RobinsonHenry Robinson, Provost of Queen's College, Oxford, 1581–1598, Bishop of Carlisle, 1598–1616. of Quenes Colledge is made bishop of Carlile, and I heare that Dr. EdesRichard Edes, Queen's Chaplain and Dean of Worcester. preaching at Oxford on Sonday last was cleane out and could go no farther, but kepe that to yourself. Master EdmondesThomas Edmondes (knighted, 1603). is either gon or presently going into Fraunce with letters to prepare the way for this peace. All that I heare of Tobie Mathew is that he stayde in Fraunce where he fell sicke of the small pockes, with younge ThrogmortonProbably a son of Thomas Throckmorton of Coughton.. Your cosen LyttonRowland Lytton. and his lady wilbe in towne this weeke. Master DormerMichael Dormer. went hence yesterday again. Sir Ant: Mildmay went last Thursday into NorthamptonshirePresumably to his house at Apethorpe. but wilbe here within this sevenight; in the mean time he bad me remember his commendations to you. Your sister Alice hath lien at your brother Williams in the country ever since St. Georges day, but I thincke she be now with her lady, for so she told me fowre dayes ago when I saw her last.

closer.salute: And this being all my store of publike or private newes, with my best wishes I bid you farewell.

address.addrLine: To my assured goode frend Master Edward Dudley Carleton attending on Sir Edward Norris Lord Governor of Ostend.

div.head: Editor's Note
div.p: Written from London on 17 May 1598, this is Chamberlain's third surviving letter to Dudley Carleton, who was then serving under Sir Edward Norris, Lord Governor of Ostend. The letter opens with domestic logistics — the difficulty of reliable letter delivery between London and the Continent, and the role of intermediaries including Master Clarke and Alice Carleton — before turning to the larger political scene. Chamberlain reports on Anglo-Spanish peace negotiations, noting that Lord Buckhurst has been appointed to travel to Abbeville to conclude terms. He also relays the deteriorating situation in Ireland, rumors of the Earl of Cumberland's naval exploits in the Atlantic (which, as McClure's note confirms, proved unfounded), the death of the Countess of Hartford, and a reported duel involving Sir Charles Blount and a Flemish knight in Paris. A violent street assault on Lord Evers and Sir William Evers by members of the Witherington family — rooted in a long-running northern border feud — occupies the third paragraph. The letter closes with a miscellany of social and personal news: a new bishopric for Dr. Robinson, an embarrassing failure by Dr. Edes in the Oxford pulpit, the movements of Tobie Mathew (ill in France), Rowland Lytton, Michael Dormer, Sir Anthony Mildmay, and the whereabouts of Alice Carleton. The letter exemplifies Chamberlain's characteristic mode as London correspondent: swift, observational, and indifferent to the boundary between high politics and social gossip.
